title: Module 1 quiz: What an autonomous assistant actually is

questions.prompt: Roughly how many levels are on the autonomy spectrum?

choices.text: Five: chatbot, tool-using assistant, initiative assistant (propose + approve), scheduled assistant (runs on time/events), background coworker (acts within trust boundaries). Pick the level by matching user delegation willingness, mistake reversibility, and eval coverage.

questions.explanation: Autonomy is a spectrum, not a binary. Most successful products live at level 3-4; level 5 is the future for narrow, well-evaluated domains.

questions.prompt: How does an assistant differ from an agent?

choices.text: Agents are task-oriented (run, complete, stop); assistants are relationship-oriented (long-term memory, schedule/event awareness, initiative across the user's day). An agent's value is per-task; an assistant's value compounds over time.

questions.explanation: Naming matters. Most products called 'AI assistants' are actually agents — and users feel oversold when the assistant traits (memory, schedule, initiative) are missing.

questions.prompt: A team launches an assistant that auto-archives 'unimportant' emails with 95% accuracy. The 5% miss causes outsized angry feedback. Why?

choices.text: Trust spending is asymmetric — surprises and destructive actions cost much more trust than success builds. 95% accuracy on a destructive auto-action can net negative trust. Switch to 'propose + approve' (level 3): same value, no trust burn.

questions.explanation: Trust budgets are asymmetric — wins build slowly, surprises burn fast. For destructive or hard-to-undo actions, even high accuracy can net trust-negative. Level 3 (propose + approve) avoids the burn.

questions.prompt: Your team scopes v1 to 'all knowledge workers' with 12 tool integrations. Right approach?

choices.text: No. Scope hard: one persona, one JTBD, 2–5 tools, level 3 autonomy. Specific personas drive specific decisions; broad v1s nearly always fail. Better to ship a great narrow assistant than a mediocre wide one; expand after v1 wins.

questions.explanation: Generalist v1s fail. Specific persona + specific JTBD + tight tool set + level 3 autonomy is the canonical scope for first-version assistants.

questions.prompt: What builds user trust most reliably over time?

choices.text: Transparency + asking before risky actions + consistency + quick undo + visible audit trail. Each successful, transparent interaction adds to the trust budget; surprises and hidden actions burn it. Earn level 5 through a long string of level 3 wins where the user always knew what was happening.

questions.explanation: Trust accrues from successful, transparent action. Hidden surprises and unrecoverable mistakes burn it fast. The path to high autonomy is paved with low-autonomy wins.
